title: Security Role Changes vs RACI in ServiceNow CMDB Ownership

compliance.assurance_rationale: ISO/IEC 27001 A.5.2 — Reviewed: SPL provides contributing evidence for this regulatory requirement. The search query directly addresses the monitoring objective specified by the clause.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.10: Security Role Changes vs RACI in ServiceNow CMDB Ownership.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.6.10' running on snow:cmdb_ci, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:cmdb_ci earliest=-90d
| where match(name,"(?i)prod)")
| stats latest(sys_updated_on) as last_update, values(managed_by) as owners by name
| lookup security_raci.csv ci_name AS name OUTPUT expected_owner
| where NOT match(mvjoin(owners,"|"), expected_owner)
description: Correlates CI ownership role changes with expected RACI contacts so responsibilities for assets remain traceable.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for ISO 27001 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes itsm and sourcetypes snow:cmdb_ci. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Maintain RACI lookup from ISO SoA; (2) normalize CMDB fields; (3) quarterly access governance review. Indexes required: `index=itsm`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=snow:cmdb_ci`.
knownFalsePositives:
1. **Organisational restructuring transitions** — Role changes during restructuring may create temporary RACI misalignment while new assignments are formalised in ServiceNow.

2. **Acting/interim appointments** — Staff in acting roles may not have formal CMDB ownership updates until the appointment is confirmed as permanent.

3. **Shared responsibility boundaries** — Security responsibilities shared between teams may not map cleanly to single-owner RACI entries.

4. **CMDB synchronisation delays** — HR system changes propagating to CMDB may take one sync cycle, creating brief ownership gaps.

5. **Contractor-to-permanent transitions** — Staff transitioning from contractor to permanent status may have ownership records in both systems temporarily.

**Operational suppression:** Maintain a lookup table (`iso27001_approved_exceptions.csv`) mapping known legitimate activities by control owner, risk acceptance reference, and expiry date. Filter the SPL results against this lookup to suppress documented exceptions and reduce alert fatigue. Review and rotate entries at each ISMS management review cycle.
detailedImplementation:
## Prerequisites

• **Splunk**: Enterprise ≥9.2 or Splunk Cloud (current). Scheduling requires a search head not under memory pressure (check `index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd component=ProcessRunner` for OOM warnings before deploying a new scheduled search).
• **Technology Add-on**: Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928) must be installed on search heads and indexers. Verify with `| rest /services/apps/local | search label="Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow" | table label version`.
• **Indexes**: `itsm` must exist on all indexers with retention matching the regulation's minimum (PCI: 1y, HIPAA: 6y, NIST 800-53: 1-3y per FIPS 199, SOX: 7y). Set `frozenTimePeriodInSecs` accordingly in `indexes.conf`.
• **Lookups**: `security_raci.csv` must be deployed to the search-tier app and refreshed on a documented schedule. For KV Store lookups, verify with `| rest /servicesNS/-/-/data/transforms/lookups | search name IN ('security_raci.csv')`.
• **Regulatory mapping**: This detection evidences clause `A.5.2`. The control objective is: "Evidence that ISO 27001 A.5.2 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.6.10: Security Role Changes vs RACI in ServiceNow CMDB Ownership." — every alert payload should include this text so downstream consumers know the compliance purpose.

## Step 1 — Configure data collection

Data collection is performed by the Splunk Add-on for ServiceNow (Splunkbase app ID 1928), landing events in `itsm` under `snow:cmdb_ci`. REST-polled ticket tables produce 500-5000 records per sync per table; at 300s interval expect ~3-8 GB/month for a mid-size service desk.

Configure the input through Settings > Data Inputs > ServiceNow Service Account. A reference `inputs.conf` stanza looks like:

```ini
[snow://{instance_slug}]
url = https://<instance>.service-now.com
table = {table_name}
interval = 300
index = itsm
sourcetype = {sourcetype}
```

The following fields are authoritative for this detection once the TA parses them: `number, state, priority, assignment_group, opened_at, closed_at, sys_updated_on, caller_id, category, subcategory`. Verify they are extracted at search time with `index=itsm earliest=-15m | fieldsummary` before scheduling the detection.


Three operational considerations, authored alongside this UC, apply:


• **Hint 1:** Maintain RACI lookup from ISO SoA. The lookup table identified here is a long-lived compliance artifact; version it in source control and track every change with a pull-request review.

• **Hint 2:** normalize CMDB fields. Field normalisation mismatches are one of the top silent-failure modes in enrichment-driven detections — invest in automated tests that assert key-match rates after every TA upgrade.

• **Hint 3:** quarterly access governance review. This is an operational consideration that influences either the detection's accuracy or its maintainability — document the approach chosen in the UC runbook.

## Step 2 — Implement the search

For "Security Role Changes vs RACI in ServiceNow CMDB Ownership," the aggregation exposes change activity — change frequency, change authorship, and change surface are the dimensions the regulation cares about, not raw events.

This search enriches raw events with context from the lookup `security_raci.csv` — the enrichment table that adds compliance-relevant business context to raw events. The raw event stream alone cannot answer the compliance question because it does not carry the authoritative business attributes (`expected_owner`) — these live in a register maintained outside the monitored system. The `| lookup` joins at search time on `ci_name`, making the search results only as accurate as the lookup's freshness.

Lookup ownership and freshness: This is the single most common failure mode for lookup-based compliance searches. The `security_raci.csv` table must be owned by a named team with a documented refresh cadence and staleness alerting. A minimum operational SLA is: (1) lookup refreshed at a cadence matching the risk domain (daily for access control, weekly for inventory, quarterly for vendor registers), (2) automated alert when the lookup has not been rewritten in 2x the refresh interval, (3) a row-count sanity check (alert if row count changes by more than 20% between refreshes — catches truncation bugs in upstream exports).

Case sensitivity: Splunk's lookup matching is case-sensitive by default. If the raw event field and the lookup key differ in case (e.g. `Account_Name=JSMITH` vs lookup `samaccountname=jsmith`), the join silently returns null. Normalize both sides with `| eval ci_name = lower(ci_name)` before the lookup, or set `case_sensitive_match = false` in the lookup's `transforms.conf` stanza. Search across the last 90 days.

**Schedule configuration** (`savedsearches.conf`):
```ini
[UC-22.6.10: Security Role Changes vs RACI in ServiceNow CMDB Ownership]
cron_schedule = 0 3 1 * *
dispatch.earliest_time = -90d
dispatch.latest_time = now
actions = summary_index
action.summary_index._name = summary_compliance
action.summary_index.marker = uc=22.6.10,reg=ISO_27001
```

## Step 3 — Validate deployment

**(a) Field extraction check:** Run the following to confirm the TA is extracting every field the detection relies on:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:cmdb_ci earliest=-1h
| fieldsummary
| where count=0
| table field
```

Any row returned is a field the TA claims to extract but isn't appearing in recent data — it's either a rename upstream, a TA version mismatch, or a data-format change.

**(b) Required-field presence:** This detection specifically needs: `last_update`, `name`, `owners`. Run:

```spl
index=itsm sourcetype=snow:cmdb_ci earliest=-1h
| head 100
| table last_update, name, owners
```

Every row should have non-null values for the fields the detection's `where` clause filters on. Nulls cause silent drops in the result set.

**(c) Lookup freshness:** The detection joins against `security_raci.csv`. Check its modification time and row count:

```spl
| inputlookup security_raci.csv
| eval _lookup_row=1
| stats count as rows
```

Compare the row count to the expected baseline; an unexpected drop is a sign the upstream export truncated.

**(e) Negative test:** Generate a synthetic 'should not alert' event using the Splunk Sample Generator or a crafted `| makeresults` event and confirm the detection does NOT fire on it. A detection that fires on everything is equivalent to a detection that fires on nothing.

## Step 4 — Operationalize (dashboard and reporting)

Create a companion dashboard titled `UC-22.6.10: ISO 27001 evidence` with the following panel layout:

**Panel 1 — Drill-through table (the key fields):** Full-width bottom row. Enable row-level drilldown to a secondary dashboard that expands each violation with its raw events, timeline, and related context. This is the panel the auditor will actually read — optimise it for scannability (avoid huge wrap-around fields).

**Panel 2 — Distribution (the key fields):** Shows the volumetric distribution across the primary dimension. Use `sort` so the worst offender is first — auditors focus on the tallest bar.


Time picker defaults: set to last 30 days with named presets for 24h, 7d, 30d, 90d, and 1y — the auditor needs all five windows without retyping. Enable scheduled PDF export for the full dashboard on the regulation's reporting cadence.

## Step 5 — Troubleshooting and regulation-specific caveats

**No results when violations exist:** Check the lookup's case sensitivity (Splunk lookups are case-sensitive by default) and null handling — a null on the input side produces a silent miss, not an error.

**Search times out:** Check the search job inspector (`Activity > Jobs > <this UC's last run> > Inspect`). The phase consuming the time (fetch, command, summary) indicates whether the issue is data-layer (too wide a window, unaccelerated datamodel), command-layer (expensive `transaction` or `eventstats`), or post-aggregation.


**ISO 27001-specific failure modes:**

• Statement of Applicability (SoA) drift: ISO 27001 requires a SoA listing which Annex A controls are applicable and which are excluded with justification. A detection for a control excluded in the SoA adds noise to the audit evidence pile; reconcile UC-to-SoA mapping annually. Maintain `iso27001_soa.csv` with current applicability.

• Context of the organisation (Cl. 4): controls calibrated for a 50-person company's threat model do not suit a 5000-person multinational's. Detection thresholds, log retention, and escalation paths must align with the organisation's ISMS scope — a single 'enterprise default' for ISO 27001 is a tell-tale sign of a cookie-cutter implementation.

• Internal audit programme (Cl. 9.2) evidence: detections that support internal audits must produce records that can be attached to audit evidence packs. Tag the alert payload with audit_cycle=YYYY-HN so evidence bundling is trivial.

• Management review inputs (Cl. 9.3): control effectiveness metrics must be reviewable at management review. A detection that only raises alerts (no trend metric) cannot be brought to the management meeting; schedule a companion report that produces trend data per control.
grandmaExplanation: We keep evidence for Security Role Changes vs RACI in ServiceNow CMDB Ownership. ISO 27001 tells us what good security management looks like, and we document that we follow it.

controlTest.positiveScenario: Grant a test user elevated access to a controlled system without a corresponding approval record in the access management system; verify the monitoring search detects the unapproved privilege within 1 hour and generates an alert with user, system, and missing approval details.
controlTest.negativeScenario: Grant a test user access following the full approval workflow with manager sign-off, risk assessment, and time-limited duration recorded; verify no access violation alert fires and the access appears as properly authorised in the dashboard.

evidence: Access governance report showing recertification completion rates, privilege changes, approval chain documentation, and anomalous access investigation outcomes.
exclusions: Approved maintenance window access; automated service account operations per register; break-glass access during declared incidents; access within recertification cycle timing; project-based temporary elevation.
